explicit_text: I was raised in Glendale, California after spending my early childhood in rural surroundings. Growing up there, my father, a nurseryman and tree-moving contractor, gave me the freedom to explore the hills and fields around Glendale. This environment sparked my curiosity and helped me develop my artistic capabilities by working with natural materials.
implicit_text: I was raised in a suburban city in Southern California after spending my early childhood in rural surroundings. Growing up there, my father, a nurseryman and tree-moving contractor, gave me the freedom to explore the hills and fields around this city. This environment sparked my curiosity and helped me develop my artistic capabilities by working with natural materials.

explicit_text: I received a tuition scholarship to the Chouinard Art Institute in 1926, where I met influential peers like Millard Sheets and Don Graham. I was honored to be chosen as a teaching assistant and even taught classes when my instructor resigned. After returning to Chouinard in 1934, I enjoyed working with a vibrant faculty and later spent twelve years there during my second tenure, producing a film called The Art of Drawing and engaging with students eager for new ideas.

explicit_text: Millard Sheets was a fellow student at Chouinard when I first attended in 1926, and he later invited me to head the drawing department at Otis Art Institute after the Jepson Art Institute closed. He was a significant figure in my career, providing opportunities and maintaining a professional relationship through the years.

explicit_text: In 1929, I won second prize in the California Water Color Society Show, which was an early recognition of my work as a fine artist. This achievement encouraged me to continue pursuing my artistic career alongside my commercial art ventures.

explicit_text: After my teaching contract at Chouinard was not renewed in 1930, I opened a custom-made furniture business in Beverly Hills. Unfortunately, the business failed due to competition from mass-produced items, which was a difficult period compounded by my marriage and the birth of my first child.

explicit_text: In 1933, while on a business trip to Chicago, I decided to attend the Chicago Art Institute to further my art education. This experience expanded my interest in art education and led to my re-invitation to teach at Chouinard in 1934. I also explored art departments in the Midwest to study their educational methods during this time.

explicit_text: During World War II, I worked at Lockheed Corporation as a toolmaker starting in 1942. I designed several time-saving devices and was promoted to senior tool designer, which gave me the financial means to seriously consider opening my own art school after the war.

explicit_text: In 1945, I founded the Jepson Art Institute near Otis and Chouinard in Los Angeles. Starting with just three instructors, including notable artists like Rico Lebrun and Howard Warshaw, the school grew rapidly. I led the institute with a philosophy of open, experimental education until financial and bureaucratic challenges forced its closure in 1953.

explicit_text: After the Jepson Art Institute closed, I briefly worked with the Kahn Art Institute before Millard Sheets invited me to head the drawing department at Otis Art Institute. I left Otis abruptly in 1958 due to dissatisfaction with salary issues but valued the experience before returning to Chouinard.
